Many cyclists, including children, use Ponsard as a quiet residential road to access Snowdon.  I see them riding on the sidewalk along the Decarie service road, because there is no cycling infrastructure there.

Many people bike to destinations on Queen Mary including grocery stores, pharmacies, and healthcare clinics.  During morning and afternoon rush hours, bikes are prohibited due to the reserved bus lanes and CSR regulations that prohibit riding between moving lanes.  These bus lanes should be modified to allow cycling, as has been done on other bike lanes in Montreal.

I see many cyclists on Victoria, including kids biking to nearby schools like Marianopolis.  Victoria is also one of the only north-south roads providing access between Snowdon and Westmount as well as St Henri.  The road is narrow with cars parked on both sides - I have seen many near misses from motorists trying to pass kids biking up the hill around the curb extensions at the crosswalk.  Many motorists ignore the 30 km/h speed limit.

My kids used the designated bike route on Terrebonne for years.  Although it was listed on the city's bike lane map, they related many unsafe encounters with impatient drivers along Terrebonne.  We tried to find alternate routes but Terrebonne was really the only efficient way to get to school on time in the morning.  Under the new configuration with separated bike lanes, my kids feel much safer.
